advaita.rendering: Among horses, know me as Uccaiḥśravas (uccaiḥśravas), born from the churning that brought forth the nectar of immortality (amṛtodbbhava). Among the lords of elephants, know me as Airāvata (airāvata), offspring of Irāvatī. Among humans, know me as the king (narādhipa) — the sovereign among men.
advaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Śaṅkara glosses uccaiḥśravas as 'aśvarāja' (king of horses), amṛtodbbhava as 'amṛtanimitta-mathanodbhava' (born from the churning undertaken for the sake of nectar), and airāvata as 'irāvatyāḥ apatyam' (offspring of Irāvatī). The terse identifications list sovereign exemplars in each class without elaboration — the dialectical method here is minimal: the Brahman-as-prāṇa logic of the prior verse already grounded the principle; these are corroborating instances.
advaita.doctrinal_note: For Advaita, each vibhūti (vibhūti) is not an independent deity-identification but a pointer toward the one Brahman appearing as the preeminent exemplar. The king, the horse, the elephant — all are Brahman's singular expression at the apex of each category.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Know me as Uccaiḥśravas, who arose from the churning of the nectar-ocean, foremost among all horses. Know me likewise as Airāvata, also born of that same amṛta-churning, foremost among all elephant-lords. And among humans, know me as the king.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Rāmānuja explicitly extends 'amṛtodbbhava' to Airāvata as well ('amṛtoddbhavam iti airāvatasya api viśeṣaṇam'), stressing that both horse and elephant share the same exalted cosmic origin-event. This double-attribution is distinctive: both proceed from Bhagavān's own sovereign act of cosmic churning.
viśiṣṭādvaita.doctrinal_note: In Viśiṣṭādvaita, Bhagavān does not merely coincide with the supreme member of each species; each vibhūti is a real expression of Bhagavān's indwelling sovereignty (antaryāmitva). The king, the horse, and the elephant are simultaneously real entities and real expressions of the one Bhagavān who pervades and sustains them.

dvaita.rendering: The verse falls within Madhva's combined commentary on 10.26–10.27. There Madhva pivots to Kapila, the ṛṣi who is Hari himself in the form of bliss-nature (sukharūpa) who sustains and dissolves the world — citing Chāndogya Upaniṣad ('prāṇo brahma, kaṃ brahma, khaṃ brahma') and Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad to ground Kapila's identity. The specific identifications of Uccaiḥśravas and Airāvata are subsumed under the overarching thesis: Hari alone is the source of all preeminence.
dvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Madhva's bhāṣya for this verse is explicitly combined with 10.26 and centers on the Kapila-identification. The cross-reference to Upaniṣadic passages (Chāndogya 4.10.5, Śvetāśvatara 5.2) and the Sāmaveda Bābhravya-śākhā establishes scriptural convergence for Hari's identity as Kapila. Direct commentary on Uccaiḥśravas and Airāvata is minimal in the supplied bhāṣya.
dvaita.doctrinal_note: Madhva's Dvaita insists that Hari (Hari) is utterly independent (svatantra) and the jīva eternally dependent (paratantra). Any preeminent being in any category — horse, elephant, king — is preeminent only by Hari's grace and in no sense identical to Hari; they are vibhūtis as expressions of his sovereigning power over dependent creation.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Uccaiḥśravas is the horse whose birth is from nectar itself — understand him as having seven fiery mouths, the aśva (aśva) born by nectar's emergence. He is Bhagavān's vibhūti as a sport-instrument in divine service (bhagavat-sevāyāṃ krīḍopayogitā). Similarly Airāvata, who was consecrated by that nectar-churning — both are to be contemplated (cintanīya). The hierarchy of preeminence is by Bhagavān's own sovereign will (tāratamyabheda aicchika), since there is but one speaker.
śuddhādvaita.bhāṣya_anchor: Vallabha glosses uccaiḥśravas as 'saptamukhāgnirūpa aśva' (the horse whose form is seven-mouthed fire) and derives 'amṛta' as 'amṛtena samudbhava' — born by means of nectar. He invokes Bhāgavata Purāṇa (Skandha 11, adhyāya 16) as confirming these vibhūti-listings. The observation 'ekavarktṛkatvāt' (because there is a single speaker, namely Bhagavān) grounds why the hierarchy of rank is Bhagavān's unconstrained will.
śuddhādvaita.doctrinal_note: In Śuddhādvaita / Puṣṭi-mārga, the vibhūtis are Kṛṣṇa's own līlā-prasāda (grace-play); they are not merely instances of cosmic preeminence but instruments of Kṛṣṇa's own delight. Contemplating them is an act of bhakti, not merely philosophical taxonomy.

bhakti.rendering: Know me as Uccaiḥśravas — the horse named 'high-eared' (uccaiḥśravasa) that arose from the churning of the ocean of milk (kṣīrābdhi-mathana) undertaken for the sake of nectar (amṛtārtha). This 'amṛtodbbhava' (born from nectar-churning) applies equally to Airāvata among elephant-lords. Among humans, know me as the king (narādhipa).
bhakti.bhāṣya_anchor: Śrīdhara Svāmī specifies 'amṛtārthaṃ kṣīrābdhi-mathanāt udbhūtam' — 'that which arose from churning the ocean of milk, undertaken for the sake of amṛta.' He explicitly notes that 'amṛtodbbhava' carries over grammatically (saṃbadhyate) to Airāvata as well, mirroring Rāmānuja's reading. The commentary is clean of HTML/JS artifacts and consistent Sanskrit prose.
bhakti.doctrinal_note: Śrīdhara's bhakti-philological voice finds devotional significance in the mythic origin-narrative: both Uccaiḥśravas and Airāvata were born at the same cosmic churning that produced nectar and Lakṣmī. To know Bhagavān as these beings is to participate meditatively in that primordial event of divine abundance.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Among horses, know me as Uccaiḥśravas, the horse born of the nectar-churning (amṛta-mathanodbhava). Among the lords of elephants, know me as Airāvata, likewise born of that nectar-churning. And among humans, know me as the king (narādhipa) — this is to be understood as supplied (anuṣajyate).
advaita-bhakti.bhāṣya_anchor: Madhusūdana uses the same double-application of 'amṛtamathanodbhava' to both Uccaiḥśravas and Airāvata, with the precise syntactic observation 'anuṣajyate' — the predicate 'know me' (vidhhi) is to be supplied for the narādhipa clause, indicating compact ellipsis in the mūla. His voice is closest to the grammatical-synthetic mode.
advaita-bhakti.doctrinal_note: Madhusūdana's synthesis: the supreme non-dual Brahman (Advaita ground) is also the beloved Kṛṣṇa who takes delight in manifesting as the most exalted in each order. Meditating on these vibhūtis is not mere metaphysics but an act of upāsanā (upāsanā) — a devotional dwelling in Brahman's self-disclosure.

so_what_questions: Why does the Gītā list kings, horses, and elephants rather than purely spiritual beings as vibhūtis — what does the inclusion of royal-political categories reveal about the text's theory of sovereignty?
Both Uccaiḥśravas and Airāvata arise from the same amṛta-churning: what is the significance of cosmic origin-event as the basis of preeminence, rather than intrinsic species-nature?
How does Rāmānuja's explicit extension of 'amṛtodbbhava' to Airāvata (absent in Śaṅkara's terse gloss) reflect each school's deeper commitment — grammatical minimalism vs. devotional fullness?
The human category yields only one vibhūti here — the king: what does this imply about the Gītā's anthropology and its understanding of what is 'most itself' in the human domain?

Madhva's bhāṣya pivots away to Kapila rather than dwelling on horse and elephant: what hermeneutical principle justifies treating a combined 10.26–10.27 unit as a single theological statement about Hari?
If every category of being has a sovereign exemplar through whom Bhagavān is to be known, what is the epistemological discipline implied — and can it be practiced without mythological literacy?
Vallabha's 'tāratamyabheda aicchika' (hierarchy by sovereign will) — how does divine voluntarism in ranking vibhūtis differ from the Advaita view that all preeminence is non-different from Brahman?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you encounter someone or something that is genuinely, unmistakably excellent in its domain — a surgeon of extraordinary precision, a river in full flood — pause and recognize that the excellence itself is Brahman's self-disclosure. Do not merely admire the person; see through them to the one ground. This is not flattery; it is a contemplative practice of seeing the aśvarāja in every arena.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Serve the excellent with full recognition that excellence is Bhagavān's own quality (guṇa) expressed in a real body. In a workplace, this means: when you defer to genuine mastery — in a craftsperson, a physician, a leader — you are performing kainkarya (service) to Bhagavān manifested there. The deference is not social convention; it is devotional act.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Acknowledge that the most accomplished person in any field is accomplished only by Hari's grace, not by autonomous excellence. This cuts both pride (in oneself) and idolatry (in another). When you meet a king or a master, recognize Hari's sovereign gift in them — but do not confuse the vessel with the source. Gratitude flows to Hari, not to the instrument.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Contemplate Kṛṣṇa's delight in excellence. When you see a thoroughbred horse, a majestic elephant, a just ruler — these are Kṛṣṇa's own play-instruments (krīḍopayogitā). Let admiration of the excellent become a doorway into the joy Kṛṣṇa takes in his own creation. This is Puṣṭi-mārga's gift: wonder at the world as Kṛṣṇa's līlā.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Build the habit of tracing excellence back to its cosmic origin. Uccaiḥśravas did not emerge from random chance — he was born at the great churning, as was Airāvata, as was nectar itself. When you encounter something magnificent, ask: what churning produced this? What long labor, what convergence of conditions? This is the bhakta's historiography of beauty.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Sit with the paradox that the very best in any domain — the finest horse, the sovereign king — is simultaneously a finite, replaceable being AND a window into the infinite. Madhusūdana's synthesis asks you to hold both without collapsing either. Practice this in relationships: honor the person fully (Kṛṣṇa-bhakti), and simultaneously see beyond the person to the non-dual ground (Advaita). Neither dissolve the person nor cling to them.

self_assessment: All 6 school renderings anchored in supplied bhāṣya prose with genuine divergence; Madhva flagged honestly as combined-verse with minimal direct commentary on horse/elephant; Śrīdhara used Sanskrit-only content from bhāṣya (no HTML artifacts present in payload); amṛtodbbhava double-attribution correctly tracked across Rāmānuja, Śrīdhara, and Madhusūdana.
